examples.explanation:
Step 1: Total sum = 20, target sum per subset = 5. Initialize subset sums = [0,0,0,0].
Step 2: Assign nums[0]=4 to subset 0: sums = [4,0,0,0].
Step 3: Assign nums[1]=3 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+3=7>5), skip; to subset 1: sums = [4,3,0,0].
Step 4: Assign nums[2]=2 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+2=6>5), skip; to subset 1 (invalid, 3+2=5, but subset not full yet), to subset 2: sums = [4,3,2,0].
Step 5: Assign nums[3]=3 to subset 0 (invalid), to subset 1 (invalid, 3+3=6>5), to subset 2 (invalid, 2+3=5, but continue), to subset 3: sums = [4,3,2,3].
Step 6: Assign nums[4]=5 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+5=9>5), to subset 1 (invalid, 3+5=8>5), to subset 2 (invalid, 2+5=7>5), to subset 3 (invalid, 3+5=8>5), backtrack to nums[3].
Step 7: Backtrack nums[3]=3: try subset 2 (valid, 2+3=5), sums = [4,3,5,0].
Step 8: Assign nums[4]=5 to subset 0 (invalid), to subset 1 (invalid), to subset 2 (invalid, 5+5=10>5), to subset 3: sums = [4,3,5,5].
Step 9: Assign nums[5]=2 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+2=6>5), to subset 1 (valid, 3+2=5), sums = [4,5,5,5].
Step 10: Assign nums[6]=1 to subset 0 (valid, 4+1=5), sums = [5,5,5,5]. All elements assigned, all subsets sum to 5, return true.
Step 1: Total sum = 10, target sum per subset = 10/3 not integer, return false immediately (pruning). No recursion needed.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(k^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively assign each element to one of K subsets, checking if all subsets sum to target. Use pruning to skip invalid assignments early. Suitable for small n but exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Array into K Equal Sum Subsets via Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution: recursively assign each element to one of K subsets, ensuring all subsets sum to target, with pruning for early termination.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning an array of integers into K non-empty subsets such that each subset has the same sum. The brute force backtracking approach explores all possible assignments of elements to subsets, which is exponential but manageable for small n. The intuition is to treat each element as being placed into one of K 'buckets' (subsets). We recursively assign elements to subsets, tracking the current sum of each subset. Pruning is crucial: if adding an element to a subset exceeds the target sum, we skip that assignment. Also, we avoid redundant assignments by not placing elements into empty subsets if previous empty subsets exist (since subsets are indistinguishable). The base case is when all elements are assigned; we check if all subsets sum to the target. The time complexity is O(K^N) in the worst case, as each element can go into any of K subsets. Space complexity is O(N) for recursion depth and storing subset sums. This approach is foundational for understanding backtracking but is inefficient for large n. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the recursive assignment function as its core subroutine. Common mistakes include not pruning early (leading to exponential blow-up), not handling empty subsets correctly (causing duplicate partitions), and missing the base case check. Real code pattern involves a recursive function that iterates over subsets for each element, with backtracking after each assignment.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Total sum = 20, target sum per subset = 5. Initialize subset sums = [0,0,0,0].
Step 2: Assign nums[0]=4 to subset 0: sums = [4,0,0,0].
Step 3: Assign nums[1]=3 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+3=7>5), skip; to subset 1: sums = [4,3,0,0].
Step 4: Assign nums[2]=2 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+2=6>5), skip; to subset 1 (invalid, 3+2=5, but subset not full yet), to subset 2: sums = [4,3,2,0].
Step 5: Assign nums[3]=3 to subset 0 (invalid), to subset 1 (invalid, 3+3=6>5), to subset 2 (invalid, 2+3=5, but continue), to subset 3: sums = [4,3,2,3].
Step 6: Assign nums[4]=5 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+5=9>5), to subset 1 (invalid, 3+5=8>5), to subset 2 (invalid, 2+5=7>5), to subset 3 (invalid, 3+5=8>5), backtrack to nums[3].
Step 7: Backtrack nums[3]=3: try subset 2 (valid, 2+3=5), sums = [4,3,5,0].
Step 8: Assign nums[4]=5 to subset 0 (invalid), to subset 1 (invalid), to subset 2 (invalid, 5+5=10>5), to subset 3: sums = [4,3,5,5].
Step 9: Assign nums[5]=2 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+2=6>5), to subset 1 (valid, 3+2=5), sums = [4,5,5,5].
Step 10: Assign nums[6]=1 to subset 0 (valid, 4+1=5), sums = [5,5,5,5]. All elements assigned, all subsets sum to 5, return true.
Step 1: Total sum = 10, target sum per subset = 10/3 not integer, return false immediately (pruning). No recursion needed.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Assignment of Elements to K Subsets
ladders.desc: The recursive function that assigns each element to a subset, with backtracking and pruning.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. It recursively attempts to place each array element into one of K subsets, backtracking when an assignment leads to a dead end. The intuition is to treat the problem as a depth-first search over the assignment space. For each element, we try placing it into each subset that can accommodate it without exceeding the target sum. Pruning is applied: if a subset's sum plus the current element exceeds the target, we skip that subset. Additionally, to avoid duplicate partitions (since subsets are unlabeled), we only place an element into an empty subset if no previous subset is empty (i.e., we fill subsets in order). The function returns true if all elements are successfully assigned and all subsets sum to the target. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(K^N) in worst case, as it explores up to K choices per element. Space complexity is O(N) for recursion stack and O(K) for storing subset sums. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called by the main function to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include not resetting subset sums after backtracking, incorrect pruning conditions, and infinite recursion due to missing base cases. Real code pattern: a recursive function with parameters for current element index and subset sums array.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with element index 0, sums = [0,0].
Step 2: Assign nums[0]=2 to subset 0: sums = [2,0].
Step 3: Recursively call for index 1: assign nums[1]=2 to subset 0 (valid, 2+2=4), sums = [4,0].
Step 4: For index 2: assign nums[2]=2 to subset 0 (invalid, 4+2=6>4), to subset 1: sums = [4,2].
Step 5: For index 3: assign nums[3]=2 to subset 0 (invalid), to subset 1 (valid, 2+2=4), sums = [4,4]. All assigned, return true.

Step 1: Start index 0, sums = [0,0].
Step 2: Assign nums[0]=1 to subset 0: sums = [1,0].
Step 3: For index 1: assign nums[1]=2 to subset 0 (valid, 1+2=3), sums = [3,0].
Step 4: For index 2: assign nums[2]=3 to subset 0 (invalid, 3+3=6>3), to subset 1: sums = [3,3]. All assigned, return true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pruning in Backtracking for Subset Sum
ladders.desc: The technique of skipping invalid assignments early to reduce search space.
ladders.explanation: Pruning is essential to make backtracking feasible by eliminating branches that cannot lead to a valid solution. The intuition is that if adding an element to a subset would cause its sum to exceed the target, there's no point exploring that path. Similarly, to avoid duplicate partitions, we restrict assignments to empty subsets: we only place an element into an empty subset if all previous subsets are non-empty (i.e., we fill subsets sequentially). This works because subsets are indistinguishable; placing an element into subset i vs. subset j (both empty) leads to the same partition. The algorithm checks these conditions before making a recursive call. Time complexity benefit: pruning can significantly reduce the number of recursive calls, though worst-case remains exponential. Space complexity is unaffected. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded within the recursive assignment function to decide which subsets to try for each element. Common mistakes include incorrect pruning conditions (e.g., not checking for empty subsets correctly) or missing pruning altogether, leading to timeouts. Real code pattern: in the recursive loop over subsets, add 'if' conditions to skip subsets where sum + nums[idx] > target or (sums[i] == 0 and i > 0 and sums[i-1] == 0).

examples.input: nums = [3,2,1], K = 2, target = 3, current sums = [3,0], idx = 1
examples.trace:
Step 1: At idx=1, nums[1]=2.
Step 2: Check subset 0: sum=3, 3+2=5 > target=3, so prune this branch, do not recurse.
Step 3: Check subset 1: sum=0, 0+2=2 <= target, and subset 1 is empty but subset 0 is non-empty, so allowed. Proceed with assignment.

examples.input: nums = [1,1,1], K = 2, target = 1.5, current sums = [0,0], idx = 0
examples.trace:
Step 1: At idx=0, nums[0]=1.
Step 2: Check subset 0: sum=0, 0+1=1 <= target, and subset 0 is empty with no previous subsets, so allowed.
Step 3: Check subset 1: sum=0, 0+1=1 <= target, but subset 1 is empty and subset 0 is also empty, so prune to avoid duplicate. Only assign to subset 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking if Array Can Be Partitioned into K Subsets with Equal Sum
ladders.desc: Validating the precondition: total sum divisible by K and all elements ≤ target sum.
ladders.explanation: Before diving into backtracking, we must check if a partition is even possible. The intuition is mathematical: for K subsets to have equal sum, the total sum of the array must be divisible by K. Additionally, no single element can exceed the target sum (total sum / K), as it would make partitioning impossible. This building block involves computing the total sum, checking divisibility, and verifying element bounds. If these checks fail, we can return false immediately without recursion, saving time. The algorithm: sum all elements, if sum % K != 0 return false; compute target = sum / K; if any element > target return false. Time complexity: O(N) for summing and scanning. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L2: This is a preliminary step that ensures the backtracking logic has a valid target to work towards. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for element > target or integer division issues. Real code pattern: a simple loop to compute sum and max element, followed by conditional checks.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute total sum = 1+2+3+4 = 10.
Step 2: Check divisibility: 10 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0, so return false. No need to check elements.

Step 1: Total sum = 20.
Step 2: 20 % 4 = 0, so target = 5.
Step 3: Check elements: all are 5 ≤ target, so precondition passed, proceed to backtracking.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Summing Elements of an Array
ladders.desc: Computing the total sum of all integers in an array.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used throughout the partition problem. The intuition is simple: we need to know the total sum to determine the target sum per subset. The algorithm iterates through each element in the array and accumulates their values into a running total. Start with sum = 0, for each element in array: sum += element. Time complexity: O(N) where N is the number of elements. Space complexity: O(1) as only a single variable is used. Connection to L3: This operation is the first step in checking preconditions for partition. Common mistakes include integer overflow (if sums are large) or off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: a for-loop or built-in sum function.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize sum = 0.
Step 2: Add nums[0]=1: sum = 1.
Step 3: Add nums[1]=2: sum = 3.
Step 4: Add nums[2]=3: sum = 6.
Step 5: Add nums[3]=4: sum = 10.

Step 1: sum = 0.
Step 2: Add 5: sum = 5.
Step 3: Add -3: sum = 2.
Step 4: Add 2: sum = 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic concept: an array is a collection of elements accessible by index.
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type. Each element has an index, starting from 0. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 holds 10, index 1 holds 20, index 2 holds 30. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient access to elements by position. Operations include reading an element (e.g., nums[0] returns 10), updating an element, and iterating over all elements using a loop. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(N) to store N elements. Connection to L4: Summing an array requires iterating through each element using indices. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., nums[3] in a 3-element array) or confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: in Python, list is used as an array; example: nums = [1,2,3]; first = nums[0].

examples.trace:
Step 1: The array has elements at indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15.
Step 2: Access index 1: return the element at position 1, which is 10.

Step 1: Start at index 0: print 100.
Step 2: Move to index 1: print 200.
Step 3: Move to index 2: print 300.
Step 4: Move to index 3: print 400.

approaches.name: DP Bitmask with Subset Sum O(n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use bitmask DP to represent subsets, where dp[mask] stores the sum modulo target. Iterate over masks to build subsets that sum to target, then count ways to partition into K subsets. Optimizes by avoiding redundant calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition into K Subsets with DP Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP bitmask to count ways to partition array into K subsets with equal sum.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning an array of integers into K subsets such that each subset has the same sum. The DP bitmask approach efficiently explores all possible partitions using bitmask representation of subsets. The intuition is to represent subsets using bitmasks where each bit indicates whether an element is included. We use dynamic programming where dp[mask] stores the sum of the selected elements modulo the target sum (total_sum/K). This allows us to track partial subsets that sum to multiples of the target. The algorithm iterates through all masks, building subsets incrementally. For each mask, we try adding an unselected element to form a new mask. If adding the element doesn't exceed the target sum, we update dp[new_mask]. If it exactly reaches the target sum, we reset to 0 (starting a new subset). Finally, we check if the full mask (all elements selected) has sum 0 modulo target, indicating successful partitioning into K subsets. The time complexity is O(n * 2^n) as we iterate over all masks and for each mask, potentially over all elements. Space complexity is O(2^n) for the dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the subset sum tracking subroutine from L1 as the core mechanism. Common mistakes include not handling the case where total_sum is not divisible by K (return 0 immediately), incorrectly resetting dp when target is reached, and inefficient bitmask iteration. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[0] = 0; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask] is defined: for i in range(n): if not (mask & (1<<i)): new_mask = mask | (1<<i); new_sum = dp[mask] + nums[i]; if new_sum < target: dp[new_mask] = new_sum; elif new_sum == target: dp[new_mask] = 0; else: break; return 1 if dp[(1<<n)-1] == 0 else 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: total_sum = 20, target = 5 (20/4). Initialize dp[0] = 0. Step 2: Process mask 0: try adding each element. For i=0 (4): new_sum=4 <5, dp[0000001]=4. i=1 (3): dp[0000010]=3. i=2 (2): dp[0000100]=2. i=3 (3): dp[0001000]=3. i=4 (5): new_sum=5 ==5, dp[0010000]=0. i=5 (2): dp[0100000]=2. i=6 (1): dp[1000000]=1. Step 3: Process mask 0010000 (element 5 alone): sum=0. Add i=0 (4): new_sum=4 <5, dp[0010001]=4. Continue similarly. Step 4: Eventually reach full mask 1111111 (all elements). Check dp[1111111]=0, indicating all subsets sum to target. Return 1.

Step 1: total_sum = 10, target = 5. Initialize dp[0]=0. Step 2: Process masks incrementally. For mask 0: add 1→dp[0001]=1, add 2→dp[0010]=2, add 3→dp[0100]=3, add 4→dp[1000]=4. Step 3: Process mask 0001 (sum=1): add 2→new_sum=3 <5, dp[0011]=3; add 3→dp[0101]=4; add 4→new_sum=5 ==5, dp[1001]=0. Step 4: Process mask 1001 (sum=0, subset {1,4}): add 2→dp[1011]=2; add 3→new_sum=3 <5, dp[1101]=3. Step 5: Process mask 1101 (sum=3): add 2→new_sum=5 ==5, dp[1111]=0. Step 6: dp[1111]=0, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Subset Sum Tracking with Bitmask DP
ladders.desc: DP bitmask to track sums of subsets modulo a target value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution. It uses dynamic programming with bitmasks to track the sum of selected elements modulo a target value. The intuition is to represent each subset of elements as a bitmask (integer where bit i is 1 if element i is included). We maintain a dp array where dp[mask] stores the current sum of the subset represented by mask, modulo the target. This allows us to build subsets incrementally: starting from empty mask (sum 0), we add one element at a time to form new masks. If adding an element causes the sum to reach exactly the target, we reset the sum to 0 for that new mask (indicating completion of one subset). If the sum exceeds the target, we stop (as elements are positive). The algorithm ensures we explore all possible subsets efficiently. Time complexity: O(n * 2^n) for iterating over all masks and elements. Space: O(2^n). Connection to L0: This subroutine directly implements the DP logic needed for partitioning. Connection to L2: It relies on the bitmask representation technique. Common mistakes: Not resetting sum to 0 when target is reached, incorrectly handling modulo, or using inefficient bitmask loops. Real code pattern: dp = [-1] * (1<<n); dp[0] = 0; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask] >= 0: for i in range(n): if not (mask & (1<<i)): new_mask = mask | (1<<i); new_sum = dp[mask] + nums[i]; if new_sum < target: dp[new_mask] = new_sum; elif new_sum == target: dp[new_mask] = 0; else: break.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3, dp size=8. Initialize dp[0]=0. Step 2: mask=0 (000): try add i=0 (1): new_sum=1<3, dp[001]=1; i=1 (2): dp[010]=2; i=2 (3): new_sum=3==3, dp[100]=0. Step 3: mask=1 (001, sum=1): add i=1 (2): new_sum=3==3, dp[011]=0; add i=2 (3): new_sum=4>3, break. Step 4: mask=2 (010, sum=2): add i=0 (1): new_sum=3==3, dp[011]=0 (overwrite); add i=2 (3): new_sum=5>3, break. Step 5: mask=4 (100, sum=0): add i=0 (1): new_sum=1<3, dp[101]=1; i=1 (2): new_sum=2<3, dp[110]=2; i=2 (3): new_sum=3==3, dp[111]=0. Final dp: [0,1,2,0,0,1,2,0].

Step 1: dp[0]=0. Step 2: mask=0: add 2→dp[001]=2; add 2→dp[010]=2; add 2→dp[100]=2. Step 3: mask=1 (sum=2): add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[011]=0; add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[101]=0. Step 4: mask=2 (sum=2): add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[011]=0 (same); add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[110]=0. Step 5: mask=4 (sum=2): add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[101]=0; add 2→new_sum=4==4, dp[110]=0. Step 6: mask=3 (011, sum=0): add 2→new_sum=2<4, dp[111]=2. Final dp: [0,2,2,0,2,0,0,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using integer bitmasks to represent and manipulate subsets of elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using bitmasks to represent subsets efficiently. A bitmask is an integer where each bit corresponds to an element: bit i is 1 if element i is in the subset, 0 otherwise. For n elements, there are 2^n possible subsets, representable by integers from 0 to (1<<n)-1. The intuition is that bitwise operations allow fast subset manipulation: checking if an element is included (mask & (1<<i)), adding an element (mask | (1<<i)), and iterating over all subsets. This representation compresses subset information into a single integer, enabling dynamic programming over subsets with O(2^n) states. The algorithm typically iterates over all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1, and for each mask, examines bits to include new elements. Time complexity for iterating over all masks and bits is O(n * 2^n). Space: O(2^n) for storing per-mask data. Connection to L1: This technique is used to implement the DP states. Connection to L3: It builds on binary representation concepts. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in bit shifts, incorrect mask iteration order, or misunderstanding bitwise operations. Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for mask in range(1<<n): for i in range(n): if mask & (1<<i): # element i is in subset; else: new_mask = mask | (1<<i).

examples.trace: Step 1: For n=3, masks from 0 to 7. Step 2: mask=0 (000): empty subset {}. Step 3: mask=1 (001): subset {A}. Step 4: mask=2 (010): {B}. Step 5: mask=3 (011): {A,B}. Step 6: mask=4 (100): {C}. Step 7: mask=5 (101): {A,C}. Step 8: mask=6 (110): {B,C}. Step 9: mask=7 (111): {A,B,C}. To check if B (index 1) is in mask 5 (101): mask & (1<<1) = 101 & 010 = 0, so not included. To add B to mask 5: new_mask = 101 | 010 = 111 (7).

Step 1: n=2, masks 0-3. Step 2: mask=0 (00): {}. Step 3: mask=1 (01): {X}. Step 4: mask=2 (10): {Y}. Step 5: mask=3 (11): {X,Y}. To iterate: for mask in range(4): print(bin(mask)). To add Y to mask 1: new_mask = 01 | 10 = 11 (3).

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming Over Subsets
ladders.desc: Using DP to solve problems by iterating over all subsets of a set.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves applying dynamic programming to problems where the state is a subset of elements. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems defined by which elements are included. We use a dp array indexed by bitmask (representing the subset) to store the optimal value for that subset. The algorithm typically initializes dp for the empty set, then iterates over all masks in increasing order of size (or any order that ensures dependencies are met). For each mask, we consider adding one element to transition to a larger mask. This allows solving problems like subset sum, traveling salesman, or partition by exploring all 2^n subsets efficiently. Time complexity is often O(n * 2^n) due to iterating over masks and elements. Space: O(2^n). Connection to L2: This uses bitmask representation for DP states. Connection to L4: It relies on iterative DP updates. Common mistakes: Incorrect iteration order leading to missing states, not handling base cases, or exponential blow-up for large n. Real code pattern: dp = [inf] * (1<<n); dp[0] = 0; for mask in range(1<<n): if dp[mask] is valid: for i in range(n): if not (mask & (1<<i)): new_mask = mask | (1<<i); dp[new_mask] = min(dp[new_mask], dp[mask] + cost).

examples.input: Set {A,B,C} with weights [1,2,3], find min weight to cover all
examples.trace: Step 1: n=3, dp size=8. dp[0]=0. Step 2: mask=0: add A→dp[001]=1; add B→dp[010]=2; add C→dp[100]=3. Step 3: mask=1 (sum=1): add B→dp[011]=3; add C→dp[101]=4. Step 4: mask=2 (sum=2): add A→dp[011]=3 (min); add C→dp[110]=5. Step 5: mask=3 (sum=3): add C→dp[111]=6. Step 6: mask=4 (sum=3): add A→dp[101]=4; add B→dp[110]=5. Step 7: Continue until dp[111]=6 (min sum to include all).

examples.input: Set {X,Y} with values [5,10], find max value subset
examples.trace: Step 1: n=2, dp size=4. dp[0]=0. Step 2: mask=0: add X→dp[01]=5; add Y→dp[10]=10. Step 3: mask=1 (5): add Y→dp[11]=15. Step 4: mask=2 (10): add X→dp[11]=15. Step 5: dp[11]=15 is max.

testCases.input: weights [1,2], dp over subsets for total weight
costs [1,1,1], dp for min cost to cover all

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterative Dynamic Programming Updates
ladders.desc: Fundamental technique of updating DP table iteratively based on previous states.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the core mechanic of dynamic programming: building a solution iteratively by updating a DP table based on previously computed states. The intuition is to solve a problem by breaking it into overlapping subproblems, storing results in a table (array), and filling the table in a systematic order so that when computing a new state, all required previous states are already computed. The algorithm typically initializes base cases (e.g., dp[0] for empty set), then uses loops to iterate through states. For each state, it considers transitions to new states by applying operations (e.g., adding an element) and updates the DP table accordingly. This ensures each subproblem is solved once, avoiding recomputation. Time complexity depends on number of states and transitions; space is linear in number of states. Connection to L3: This is how DP over subsets is implemented. Connection to L5: It builds on array manipulation. Common mistakes: Incorrect update order (e.g., overwriting values), missing base cases, or not handling boundaries. Real code pattern: dp = [0] * (num_states); dp[0] = base_value; for i in range(num_states): if dp[i] is valid: for transition in transitions: new_state = compute_new(i, transition); dp[new_state] = update(dp[new_state], dp[i] + cost).

examples.input: DP to compute Fibonacci numbers iteratively
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1. Step 2: For i from 2 to n: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]. Step 3: Example n=5: dp[2]=1, dp[3]=2, dp[4]=3, dp[5]=5. Each state uses previous two.

examples.input: DP to compute min coins for amount
examples.trace: Step 1: dp size=8, dp[0]=0, others=inf. Step 2: For amount from 1 to 7: for coin in coins: if coin <= amount: dp[amount] = min(dp[amount], dp[amount-coin]+1). Step 3: amount=1: dp[1]=1. amount=2: dp[2]=1. amount=3: dp[3]=2. amount=4: dp[4]=2. amount=5: dp[5]=1. amount=6: dp[6]=2. amount=7: dp[7]=2.

testCases.input: Base case dp[0]=0 for sum problem
Update dp[5] using dp[3] and dp[4]
dp for step counting, steps 1 or 2, n=3
Transition from state 2 to 5 with cost 3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Fundamental programming concepts: arrays, loops, and basic operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the simplest prerequisites: using arrays (or lists) to store data and loops to iterate over them. An array is a collection of elements accessed by index. Loops (like for or while) allow repeating operations for each element. The intuition is that many algorithms process data sequentially or repeatedly, and arrays provide efficient storage and access. Basic operations include initializing an array, accessing elements by index, updating values, and iterating with a loop to perform calculations (e.g., sum, max). This is essential for implementing any DP table, as DP often uses arrays to store states. Time complexity for simple iteration is O(n) for n elements; space is O(n) for the array. Connection to L4: Arrays are used to implement DP tables. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loops, incorrect array sizes, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [0] * n; for i in range(n): arr[i] = value; or for element in arr: process(element).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: Iterate i from 0 to 3: i=0: sum=0+3=3; i=1: sum=3+1=4; i=2: sum=4+4=8; i=3: sum=8+2=10. Step 3: Return 10.
Step 1: Initialize max_val = first element =5. Step 2: Iterate i from 1 to 3: i=1: 2 <5, no change; i=2: 9 >5, max_val=9; i=3: 1 <9, no change. Step 3: Return 9.

approaches.name: DP Bitmask with Partition Count O(K * n * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Extend bitmask DP to track the number of partitions formed. dp[mask][k] stores if mask can be partitioned into k subsets with equal sum. Fill DP table by adding elements to existing subsets or starting new ones. More direct for K subsets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Partition Array into K Equal Sum Subsets
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP bitmask with partition count tracking
ladders.explanation: This problem requires partitioning an array into K subsets with equal sums. The DP bitmask approach with partition count extends the classic subset sum DP to track how many partitions have been formed. We define dp[mask][k] as a boolean indicating whether the elements represented by mask can be partitioned into k subsets with equal sum. The mask is a bitmask where bit i is 1 if nums[i] is included. The target sum per subset is total_sum/K. We initialize dp[0][0] = true (empty set forms 0 subsets). For each mask, we iterate through elements not in mask, adding them to existing subsets if they don't exceed target, or starting new subsets. The recurrence: dp[mask|(1<<i)][k] = true if dp[mask][k] and current subset sum + nums[i] ≤ target, OR dp[mask][k-1] and nums[i] ≤ target (starting new subset). We track current subset sums using an array sum[mask]. Time: O(K * n * 2^n) from nested loops over masks, K, and elements. Space: O(K * 2^n) for DP table. The intuition: we're building subsets incrementally while counting how many complete subsets we've formed, ensuring each reaches exactly target sum. Connection to L1: This uses the subset sum DP with partition tracking as the core subroutine. Common mistakes: forgetting to check divisibility (total_sum % K == 0), not handling empty subsets properly, or incorrect transition logic between partition counts. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[0][0]=True, sum array zeros. For mask in range(1<<n): For k in range(K+1): If dp[mask][k]: For i not in mask: new_mask=mask|(1<<i); new_sum=sum[mask]+nums[i]; If new_sum ≤ target: dp[new_mask][k]=True; sum[new_mask]=new_sum; If new_sum==target: dp[new_mask][k+1]=True; sum[new_mask]=0. Return dp[(1<<n)-1][K].

examples.trace: Total sum=20, target=5. Initialize dp[0][0]=True, sum[0]=0. Mask 0: dp[0][0]=True, try adding each element: Add 4 → mask=0000001 (binary), sum=4 ≤5, dp[0000001][0]=True, sum[0000001]=4. Similarly for others. Progress through masks: e.g., mask 0000011 (elements 4,3): sum=7 >5, skip. Mask 0000101 (4,2): sum=6 >5. Eventually find mask 1111111 (all elements) with k=4: Subsets: [4,1]=5, [3,2]=5, [3,2]=5, [5]=5. DP builds this: After processing, dp[1111111][4]=True.

Total sum=10, target=10/3 not integer → return false early. If divisible check passed, DP would attempt: Initialize. Try partitions: e.g., subset sums must be 10/3≈3.33, impossible with integers. DP would explore masks but never reach dp[1111][3]=True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Subset Sum DP with Partition Count Tracking
ladders.desc: DP bitmask that tracks how many complete subsets have been formed
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine solves: given a set of numbers and a target sum, can we partition them into exactly k subsets each summing to target? It extends subset sum DP by adding a dimension for partition count. We maintain dp[mask][k] where mask represents selected elements, and k is number of complete subsets formed. Additionally, we track sum[mask] = current sum of the 'in-progress' subset for that mask. Transitions: from state (mask, k), we can add an element i not in mask to the current subset if sum[mask] + nums[i] ≤ target, updating sum. If after adding, the sum equals target, we can increment k to k+1 and reset sum to 0 (starting a new subset). The base case: dp[0][0] = true with sum[0]=0. The intuition: we're building subsets one element at a time, and whenever a subset hits the target, we count it as complete and start fresh. This avoids explicitly storing which elements go in which subset. Time: O(k * n * 2^n) for iterating masks, k, and elements. Space: O(k * 2^n). Connection to L0: This is the core DP used in the full problem; L0 adds input handling and returns the final result. Common mistakes: incorrect transition when sum equals target (must increment k and reset sum), or not handling the case where sum exceeds target. Real code pattern: dp=[[False]*(K+1) for _ in range(1<<n)]; sum_arr=[0]*(1<<n); dp[0][0]=True. For mask in range(1<<n): For k in range(K+1): If dp[mask][k]: curr_sum=sum_arr[mask]; For i in range(n): If not (mask>>i &1): new_mask=mask|(1<<i); new_sum=curr_sum+nums[i]; If new_sum ≤ target: dp[new_mask][k]=True; sum_arr[new_mask]=new_sum; If new_sum==target: dp[new_mask][k+1]=True; sum_arr[new_mask]=0.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0][0]=True, sum[0]=0. Mask 0: k=0, try i=0: new_mask=001, new_sum=2==target → dp[001][1]=True, sum[001]=0. From mask 001, k=1: try i=1: new_mask=011, new_sum=2==target → dp[011][2]=True, sum[011]=0. From mask 011, k=2: try i=2: new_mask=111, new_sum=2==target → dp[111][3]=True, sum[111]=0. Result dp[111][3]=True.

Initialize. Mask 0: try i=0: new_mask=001, new_sum=1<3 → dp[001][0]=True, sum[001]=1. From mask 001, k=0: try i=1: new_mask=011, new_sum=3==target → dp[011][1]=True, sum[011]=0. From mask 011, k=1: try i=2: new_mask=111, new_sum=3==target → dp[111][2]=True, sum[111]=0. But need K=2? Wait, we have dp[111][2]=True? Let's check: Actually from mask 011, k=1, adding i=2 gives new_sum=3==target, so dp[111][2]=True. So output should be true? But subsets: [1,2] and [3] sum to 3 each. Yes, true. Let's correct: For false example, use nums=[1,2,3], target=3, K=3 → total sum=6, need 3 subsets of sum 2, impossible. Trace: dp[111][3] never becomes True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask DP for Subset Sum with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Using bitmask to represent subsets and DP to track achievable sums and counts
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using bitmask DP to solve subset problems with additional state. A bitmask is an integer where each bit represents whether an element is included (1) or not (0). For n elements, masks range from 0 to 2^n -1. We use DP where dp[mask] stores information about the subset represented by mask—in this case, a pair (current_sum, partitions_count). The transition: from a mask, we can add an element i not in mask to form a new mask, updating the state. The key insight is that by tracking current_sum and partitions_count, we can build subsets incrementally without storing explicit assignments. The intuition: think of exploring all subsets via bitmask, and for each, we know the sum of the 'last' subset being built and how many complete subsets we have. This allows us to combine elements flexibly. Time: O(n * 2^n) for iterating masks and elements. Space: O(2^n). Connection to L1: This is the foundational DP technique; L1 adds the specific application to partition counting. Common mistakes: not iterating masks in increasing order (should iterate all masks), or incorrect state updates. Real code pattern: dp = [None] * (1<<n); dp[0] = (0,0). For mask in range(1<<n): If dp[mask] is not None: curr_sum, partitions = dp[mask]; For i in range(n): If not (mask>>i &1): new_mask = mask | (1<<i); new_sum = curr_sum + nums[i]; Update dp[new_mask] based on new_sum and target.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0]=(0,0). Mask 0: try i=0 → new_mask=001, new_sum=1<3 → dp[001]=(1,0). i=1 → new_mask=010, new_sum=2<3 → dp[010]=(2,0). i=2 → new_mask=100, new_sum=3==target → dp[100]=(0,1). Mask 001 (sum=1, parts=0): try i=1 → new_mask=011, new_sum=3==target → dp[011]=(0,1). i=2 → new_mask=101, new_sum=4>3 skip. Continue for all masks.

dp[0]=(0,0). Mask 0: i=0 → new_mask=01, new_sum=2==target → dp[01]=(0,1). i=1 → new_mask=10, new_sum=2==target → dp[10]=(0,1). Mask 01: i=1 not in mask → new_mask=11, new_sum=2==target → dp[11]=(0,2).

ladders.title: Building Block — Bitmask Representation of Subsets
ladders.desc: Using integers as bitmasks to represent subsets of elements
ladders.explanation: A bitmask is an integer where each bit corresponds to an element in a set. For a set of size n, we use an n-bit integer: bit i (0-indexed) is 1 if element i is in the subset, 0 otherwise. This allows efficient representation and manipulation of subsets. Key operations: checking if element i is in mask: (mask >> i) & 1; adding element i to mask: mask | (1 << i); iterating over all masks from 0 to 2^n -1 covers all subsets. The intuition: instead of storing lists or sets, we use a single integer, enabling fast DP transitions and minimal memory. This is crucial for DP over subsets because 2^n subsets can be enumerated compactly. Time to iterate all masks: O(2^n). Space: O(1) per mask. Connection to L2: Bitmask is the data structure used in the DP; L2 builds DP on top of it. Common mistakes: off-by-one in bit indices, or misunderstanding mask values (e.g., mask 0 represents empty set). Real code pattern: n = len(nums); for mask in range(1 << n): # iterate all subsets; for i in range(n): if (mask >> i) & 1: # element i is in subset; new_mask = mask | (1 << i) # add element i.

examples.trace: Mask 4 in binary: 100. Element index 1 means set bit 1 to 1. 1<<1 = 010. mask | 010 = 100 | 010 = 110 (binary), which is 6 decimal. Represents {B,C} if original mask 4 was {C}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over All Subsets Using Bitmask
ladders.desc: Looping from 0 to 2^n -1 to generate all subsets of n elements
ladders.explanation: To explore all subsets of a set with n elements, we iterate an integer mask from 0 to 2^n - 1 inclusive. Each mask's binary representation corresponds to a unique subset: bit i is 1 if element i is in the subset. This generates all 2^n subsets efficiently. The intuition: counting in binary from 0 to 2^n-1 naturally enumerates all combinations of n bits, each representing a subset. This is fundamental for DP over subsets because it allows systematic exploration. Time: O(2^n) iterations. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This iteration is how we use bitmasks in algorithms; L3 defines the bitmask itself. Common mistakes: using range(2^n) without the -1 (if n>0, 2^n-1 is the max), or misunderstanding that mask values are integers not bit arrays. Real code pattern: n = len(set); for mask in range(1 << n): # 1<<n computes 2^n; process subset represented by mask.

examples.trace: 1<<3 = 8. range(8) = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]. Binary from 000 to 111, representing all subsets of 3 elements.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bitwise Operations
ladders.desc: Understanding binary representation and bitwise operators for beginners
ladders.explanation: Binary numbers are base-2 numbers using digits 0 and 1. Each digit is a bit. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operators manipulate bits: AND (&) outputs 1 if both bits are 1; OR (|) outputs 1 if at least one bit is 1; XOR (^) outputs 1 if bits differ; left shift (<<) shifts bits left, multiplying by 2; right shift (>>) shifts bits right, dividing by 2. For example, 1 << 3 means shift 1 left by 3 bits: binary 1 becomes 1000 (decimal 8). The intuition: we can use bits to represent true/false states, like whether an element is in a set. This is efficient because integers store multiple bits compactly. Time for operations: O(1). Space: O(1) per integer. Connection to L4: Bitwise operations enable iterating subsets; L4 uses << to compute 2^n. Common mistakes: confusing bitwise AND with logical AND, or misusing shift operators. Real code pattern: a = 5 # binary 101; b = 3 # 011; a & b = 1 (001); a | b = 7 (111); a << 1 = 10 (1010); a >> 1 = 2 (010).

examples.trace: 6 in binary: 110. 3 in binary: 011. AND operation: bitwise: 1&0=0, 1&1=1, 0&1=0 → result 010, which is decimal 2.
1 in binary: 1. Shift left by 4 bits: add 4 zeros to the right: 1 becomes 10000 in binary. 10000 binary is 1*16 = 16 decimal.
